analogy: Think of "Parliament and the Making of Laws" through the chapter's own opening images: 1 – Robert Clive accepting the Diwani of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa from the Mughal ruler in 1765 The Company Becomes the Diwan On 12 August 1765, the Mughal emperor appointed the East India Company as the Diwan of Bengal. The actual event most probably... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: RULING THE COUNTRYSIDE 27, 28 OUR PASTS – III, RULING THE COUNTRYSIDE 29, 30 OUR PASTS – III. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Parliament and the Making of Laws is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 1 – Robert Clive accepting the Diwani of Bengal, Bihar and Orissa from the Mughal ruler in 1765 The Company Becomes the Diwan On 12 August 1765, the Mughal emperor appointed the East India Company as the Diwan of Bengal.

The text explains that the actual event most probably took place in Robert Clive’s tent, with a few Englishmen and Indians as witnesses.

The text explains that but in the painting above, the event is shown as a majestic occasion, taking place in a grand setting.

The text explains that the painter was commissioned by Clive to record the memorable events in Clive’s life.

The text explains that the grant of Diwani clearly was one such event in British imagination.

The text explains that a s D i w a n , t h e C o m p a n y b e c a m e t h e c h i e f f i n a n c i a l administrator of the territory under its control.

The text explains that now it had to think of administering the land and organising its revenue resources.

The text explains that this had to be done in a way that could yield enough revenue to meet the growing expenses of the company.

The text explains that a trading company had also to ensure that it could buy the products it needed and sell what it wanted.

The text explains that rULING THE COUNTRYSIDE 27 Over the years, the Company also learnt that it had to move with some caution.

The text explains that being an alien power, it needed to pacify those who in the past had ruled the countryside, and enjoyed authority and prestige.

The text explains that those who had held local power had to be controlled but they could not be entirely eliminated.

The text explains that in this chapter we will see how the Company came to colonise the countryside, organise revenue resources, redefine the rights of people, and produce the crops it wanted.

The text explains that revenue for the Company The Company had become the Diwan, but it still saw itself primarily as a trader.

The text explains that it wanted a large revenue income but was unwilling to set up any regular system of assessment and collection.

The text explains that the effort was to increase the revenue as much as it could and buy fine cotton and silk cloth as cheaply as possible.

The text explains that soon it was clear that the Bengal economy was facing a deep crisis.

The text explains that 28 OUR PASTS – III The need to improve agriculture If the economy was in ruins, could the Company be certain of its revenue income?

The text explains that after two decades of debate on the question, the Company finally introduced the Permanent Settlement in 1793.

The text explains that read the preceding pages and suggest possible reasons.

The text explains that rULING THE COUNTRYSIDE 29 On the other hand, in the villages, the cultivator found the system extremely oppressive.

The text explains that the rent he paid to the zamindar was high and his right on the land was insecure.

The text explains that a new system is devised By the early nineteenth century, many of the Company officials were convinced that the system of revenue had to be changed again.

The text explains that in the North Western Provinces of the Bengal Presidency (most of this area is now in Uttar Pradesh), an Englishman called Holt Mackenzie devised the new system which came into effect in 1822.

The text explains that he felt that the village was an important social institution in north Indian society and needed to be preserved.

The text explains that under his directions, collectors went from village to village, inspecting the land, measuring the fields, and recording the customs and rights of different groups.

The text explains that this system came to be known as the mahalwari settlement.

The text explains that the Munro system In the British territories in the South, there was a similar move away from the idea of Permanent Settlement.

The text explains that the new system that was devised came to be known as the ryotwar (or ryotwari ).

The text explains that subsequently developed by Thomas Munro, this system was gradually extended all over south India.

The text explains that the settlement, they argued, had to be made directly with the cultivators (ryots ) who had tilled the land for generations.

The text explains that all was not well Within a few years after the new systems were imposed, it was clear that all was not well with them.

The text explains that optimistic officials had imagined that the new systems would transform the peasants into rich enterprising farmers.

The text explains that on the right is a floral cotton print designed and produced by William Morris, a famous poet and artist of nineteenth-century Britain.

The text explains that worried by the competition from indigo, woad producers in Europe pressurised their governments to ban the import of indigo.

The text explains that by the seventeenth century, European cloth producers persuaded their governments to relax the ban on indigo import.

The text explains that while the demand for indigo increased, its existing supplies from the West Indies and America collapsed for a variety of reasons.

The text explains that cloth dyers in Britain now desperately looked for new sources of indigo supply.

The text explains that there were two main systems of indigo cultivation – nij and ryoti.

The text explains that within the system of nij cultivation, the planter produced indigo in lands that he directly controlled.

The text explains that he either bought the land or rented it from other zamindars and produced indigo by directly employing hired labourers.

The text explains that less than 25 per cent of the land producing indigo was under this system.

The text explains that the rest was under an alternative mode of cultivation – the ryoti system.

The text explains that indigo on the land of ryots Under the ryoti system, the planters forced the ryots to sign a contract, an agreement (satta).

The text explains that peasants who were initially tempted by the loans soon realised how harsh the system was.

The text explains that clearly, the indigo system was intensely oppressive.

The text explains that the indigo peasants also imagined that the British government would support them in their struggle against the planters.

The text explains that after the Revolt of 1857, the British government was particularly worried about the possibility of another popular rebellion.

The text explains that the ryots saw the tour as a sign of government sympathy for their plight.

The text explains that with the discovery of synthetic dyes in the late nineteenth century, their business was severely affected, but yet they managed to expand production.

The text explains that when Mahatma Gandhi returned from South Africa, a peasant from Bihar persuaded him to visit Champaran and see the plight of the indigo cultivators there.

The text explains that mahatma Gandhi’s visit in 1917 marked the beginning of the Champaran movement against the indigo planters.

Source: NCERT Class 8 Social and Political Life III, Chapter 3: Parliament and the Making of Laws

prelims_traps: Do not treat RULING THE COUNTRYSIDE 27 and 28 OUR PASTS – III as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Parliament and the Making of Laws; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Parliament and the Making of Laws through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use RULING THE COUNTRYSIDE 27 and 28 OUR PASTS – III to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
